musicalLanguage.description: "Don't Stop Believin'" (1981) opens with one of rock's most recognizable piano vamps, and the harness's exact-repeat detection confirms just how literal that repetition is: measures 2-6 recur byte for byte at measures 10-14, again at 18-22, and again at 34-38 — the same figure restated four times before the song moves on. The key field here has a corrected history: this transcription originally carried an E major label, but the content strongly indicates C# major instead (margin 0.181, while the old E major label actually fits the content negatively at -0.099), so the key field has been corrected to Db major — the flat respelling of that same detected C# major. This annotation follows that corrected key, keeping the windows' sharp spellings: the confidence-gated windows are dominated by C#, F#, and F#maj7, with C#maj7, Fm7, G#, and Fm appearing as secondary colors. At 130 measures and a driving 118 BPM in 4/4, the piano part does something unusual at the very end: after measures of steady activity, it drops to complete silence for the final four measures (127-130) — the piano's own written part stops before the transcription's measure count does.
musicalLanguage.structure: The opening vamp (measures 2-38, an 8-measure cell) repeats four times, each occurrence identical to the last. A busier middle stretch (measures 28-32) reaches the piece's highest density (23-32 onsets per measure). A second riff-based block (measures 60-123) is built almost entirely from short, strongly repeating right- and left-hand figures (graded strong, compression ratios above 4.4). The final four measures (127-130) fall completely silent in this transcription.
keyMoments: The key field here has a corrected history: this transcription originally carried an E major label, but content indicates C# major at a 0.181 margin, and the field has been corrected to Db major (enharmonically the same key detection found). Every chord name in this annotation follows that C# reading.

Measures 2-6 recur identically at measures 10-14, 18-22, and 34-38 — the harness's exact-repeat detection confirms the famous opening piano vamp really is repeated four times note for note, not just similar-sounding.
Measures 28-32 are the busiest in the piece (23-32 onsets each), a genuine density peak inside the vamp-based opening section rather than at the song's later riff-driven passages.

The section lens flags measures 126-130 with by far the largest novelty score anywhere in this piece (17.3) — the piano drops to total silence for the final four measures right after sustained activity, and sudden silence registers as maximal structural change.
teachingGoals: Practice verifying a key label against the actual content before trusting it — this piece is its own worked example: its detection margin (0.181) strongly favors C# major over the E major label the transcription originally carried, and the key field has since been corrected to Db major.

Learn the opening vamp (measures 2-6) until it is completely automatic — it repeats four times in a row through measure 38, so fluency here pays off across more than a quarter of the piece's total length.
Practice sustaining the busiest measures (28-32, 23-32 onsets) without losing tempo — this is the piece's real technical peak, arriving early rather than at the end.

Recognize when a written part actually falls silent (measures 127-130) rather than assuming a transcription gap — practice the piece knowing exactly where the piano stops, so the ending doesn't come as a surprise.
styleTips: Listen for C# and F#maj7 resolving the way a tonic and subdominant would — the E major label this transcription originally carried claimed otherwise, and the key field has since been corrected to Db major. Trust what your ear and the confidence-gated windows agree on.
Play the repeating opening vamp (measures 2-38) with subtle variation in touch across its four repetitions — mechanically identical notes don't have to sound mechanically identical.
Feel the density peak at measures 28-32 as a genuine arrival, not just more notes to get through — let the extra onsets register as intensity, not just busyness.
Try letting your hands actually stop at measure 127 rather than fading out gradually — the piano part's silence here is total and immediate, not a decrescendo.
